name: Ray-Ban Meta Smart Glasses
short_description: AI-enabled smart glasses with cameras, microphones, speakers, and a multimodal assistant.
long_description: Head-worn glasses that pair a familiar eyewear form with first-person cameras, open-ear audio, and Meta AI. They make the case that an everyday accessory can carry capture, assistance, and situated AI without reading as a 'device', which is also what drives their privacy debate.

images.caption: Ray-Ban Meta (Gen 1) smart glasses with charging case.
images.alt_text: A pair of black Ray-Ban Meta Gen 1 smart glasses resting in front of their brown leather charging case.

interactions.title: Live translation in your ear
interactions.description: Talk with someone speaking Spanish, French, or Italian and hear what they say rendered into English through the open-ear speakers in near real time, with a transcript on your phone.
interactions.source_label: Meta — v11 update (Live AI, translation, Shazam)

interactions.title: Ask what you're looking at
interactions.description: Say "Hey Meta, look and..." to have the glasses snap a photo and answer questions about whatever is in front of you, from a poster to the contents of your fridge, hands-free.

interactions.title: Call a sighted volunteer by voice
interactions.description: A blind or low-vision wearer says "Hey Meta, call a volunteer on Be My Eyes" and a sighted helper sees the live first-person feed and talks them through a task over the open-ear speakers.

interactions.title: Hands-free recipe coaching
interactions.description: Prop the glasses on while cooking, follow a recipe step by step, and ask Meta AI questions out loud without touching a screen with messy hands.

interactions.title: Remember where you parked
interactions.description: Say "Hey Meta, remember where I parked" to stash the car's location, then "remind me where I parked" later — using the glasses as an everyday memory aid for parking spots and flight details.

interactions.title: Doxxing strangers with a glance
interactions.description: Two Harvard students wired the glasses' camera to face-search and public-records lookups ("I-XRAY") so that looking at a stranger surfaced their name, address, and phone number in real time — built as a warning, not a product.

interactions.title: Recording people who don't know
interactions.description: Because the glasses look like ordinary eyewear, wearers capture others in private moments; contractors reviewing the footage to train AI reported seeing people undressing or using toilets, drawing regulator scrutiny.

interactions.title: Spot-narrate a museum
interactions.description: Glance at an artwork and get a quiet plain-language explanation in your ear — grounded in the same on-board camera and multimodal assistant the glasses already use for "look and ask."

interactions.title: Read a foreign menu back to you
interactions.description: Look down at a printed menu in a language you don't read and have Meta AI describe the dishes aloud, combining the camera's text recognition with the open-ear audio it already drives.
